coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots as fluorescent probes exhibit brilliant sensitivity and selectivity in copper ion detection, which greatly depends on their microstructure. Herein, a mild oxidation method was adopted to tailor the organic macromolecules of coal and its derivatives for preparing coal-based carbon dots. Such coal-based carbon dots comprise a carbonaceous center dominated by sp2 microcrystalline carbon, surface defects formed by sp3 amorphous carbon, and oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups. The coal-based humic acid-based carbon dots (HA-CDs) exhibit high dispersity (∼7.6 nm), high sp2 microcrystalline carbon (61.73 %), abundant carboxyl groups (2.09 %), hydroxy groups (8.93 %), and pyridinic nitrogen (1.06 %). Due to their unique microstructure, the HA-CDs in aqueous solutions exhibit prominent fluorescence stability under different pH conditions and show superior selectivity and sensitivity to Cu2+ even in the existence of other metal ions. Within the concentration range of 0–40 µmol/L, the fluorescence intensity of HA-CDs linearly correlates with the concentration of Cu2+. The limit of detection is 0.014 µmol/L, allowing for tracing the Cu2+ concentration in flowing water. This work affords a novel idea for the microstructure design of high-sensitivity carbon dots for Cu2+ detection in the water environment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a mild oxidation method was adopted to tailor the organic macromolecular of coal and its derivatives for preparing coal-based carbon dots.

Carbon dots as fluorescent probes exhibit brilliant sensitivity and selectivity in copper ion detection, which greatly depends on their microstructure. Herein, a mild oxidation method was adopted to tailor the organic macromolecules of coal and its derivatives for preparing coal-based carbon dots. Such coal-based carbon dots comprise a carbonaceous center dominated by sp2 microcrystalline carbon, surface defects formed by sp3 amorphous carbon, and oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups. The coal-based humic acid-based carbon dots (HA-CDs) exhibit high dispersity (∼7.6 nm), high sp2 microcrystalline carbon (61.73 %), abundant carboxyl groups (2.09 %), hydroxy groups (8.93 %), and pyridinic nitrogen (1.06 %). Due to their unique microstructure, the HA-CDs in aqueous solutions exhibit prominent fluorescence stability under different pH conditions and show superior selectivity and sensitivity to Cu2+ even in the existence of other metal ions. Within the concentration range of 0–40 µmol/L, the fluorescence intensity of HA-CDs linearly correlates with the concentration of Cu2+. The limit of detection is 0.014 µmol/L, allowing for tracing the Cu2+ concentration in flowing water. This work affords a novel idea for the microstructure design of high-sensitivity carbon dots for Cu2+ detection in the water environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution has aroused widespread concern, especially the excessive content of heavy metals such as chromium, lead, iron, and copper in drinking water, which causes serious harm to human health [1]. Among heavy metal ions, copper ion (Cu2+) is of concern as an essential metal element for the human body, but excessive intake can lead to serious diseases like Wilson’s disease [2], Alzheimer’s [3], and Parkinson’s disease [4]. As stated by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA), the maximum allowable concentration of Cu2+ in drinking water should not exceed 20 μmol/L, thus effective and rapid monitoring of Cu2+ in the water environment is of great significance to prevent its concentration from exceeding the permissible limit.

At present, various methods have been reported to detect Cu2+, including atomic absorption spectrometry, electrochemical sensors, and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [5]. Compared with other instrument-based analytical techniques, the fluorometric detection method is attracting much attention due to its high sensitivity and selectivity, low cost, and user-friendly. Carbon dots (CDs), a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with remarkable fluorescent properties, low poisonousness, good biocompatibility, and superior stability [6], are considered the most suitable material for fluorescence detection [7,8]. To date, many studies have confirmed the practicability of using CDs as probes for detecting Cu2+. Dong et al. synthesized CDs by using acrylamide, ammonium persulfate, and etidronate acid through hydrothermal carbonization, in which the as-prepared CDs could achieve specific recognition for Cu2+ [9]. Sonaimuthu et al. synthesized multiple heteroatom-doped CDs using 2,4-diaminob-enzenesulfonic acid and phenylboronic acid as carbon sources, where the as-prepared CDs exhibited high selective quenching for Cu2+ [10]. The studies mentioned above indicate that CDs as fluorescent probes have significant selectivity and sensitivity to Cu2+. However, most CDs are usually synthesized from small organic molecules, resulting in the problems of high cost, high toxicity [9], and low yield of CDs. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore a novel, low-cost, and efficient way to prepare CDs in order to promote their large-scale application in fluorescence detection.

Coal and its derivatives, as three-dimensional organic macromolecules, consist of numerous aromatic structural units cross-linked with each other through alkyl or heteroatomic functional groups, which can be thought of as a multitude of CDs of varying sizes cross-linked to each other by bridge bonds [11–13]. Therefore, the application studies related to the preparation of coal-based CDs using coal and its derivatives as carbon sources have also been further explored. For instance, Hu et al. obtained CDs from coal through carbonization and nitric acid etching, enabling selective detection of Cu2+ in the presence of other metal ions [14]. Li et al. obtained CDs by shearing anthracite and proposed the defects caused by oxygen-containing functional groups in CDs determined the selectivity for Cu2+ [15]. Chong et al. prepared CDs derived from Long-Flame coal in two steps and evaluated the sensitivity of CDs to Cu2+ [16]. Liu et al. prepared CDs in a strong basic solution using coal-based humic acid and found the chelating interactions between the carboxylate group of CDs and Cu2+ led to fluorescence quenching [17]. These studies show that coal-based CDs prepared by tailoring the macromolecules of coal and its derivatives can be used as fluorescent probes for the detection of Cu2+, but their large-scale application is hindered by the complex preparation process and the use of concentrated acids. In addition, the effect of the microstructure of coal-based CDs on the Cu2+ detection mechanism remains to be further elucidated.

Herein, a simple, eco-friendly, and mild oxidation method has been proposed for synthesizing coal-based CDs by tailoring the organic macromolecules of coal-based humic acid, lignite, and bituminous coal based on the basic understanding that the structural units with condensed aromatic rings as the core in coal are classified as “natural” CDs. This study explored the fluorescence stability of coal-based CDs under varied pH conditions and the selectivity and sensitivity of the fluorescence of coal-based CDs to various metal ions, demonstrated that the as-prepared CDs show a good fluorescence stability and superior selectivity to Cu2+; elucidated the relationship between the microstructural characteristics of coal-based CDs derived from different precursors and their performance in the detection of Cu2+ and postulated the plausible fluorescence detection mechanism. This work is conducive to low-cost and eco-friendly production of coal-based CDs and provides an innovative viewpoint on the rational design of advanced carbon dots-based sensors.

Coal-based humic acid was purchased, purified, and deashed for use. Lignite and bituminous coal originated from Inner Mongolia and Shanxi, respectively. All samples were sieved with a 200 mesh sieve. According to national standards, the purified samples underwent both proximate analysis and ultimate analysis, as detailed in Table 1 . The chemicals used in the study included formic acid (HCOOH, ≥ 99 %), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 36 %-38 %), CrCl3, K2Cr2O7, FeCl3, BaCl2, MnCl2, CuCl2, MgSO4, Zn (CH3COO)2, PbCl2, K2SO4, NiCl2, and CoSO4, all of which were of analytical grade. Additionally, dialysis bags (100 Da, United Carbide Co. Ltd.) and microporous membranes (0.45 μm, Tianjin Jinteng Experimental Equipment Co., Ltd.) were also applied in the study. All reagents were prepared using pure water.

Coal-based CDs were synthesized from three types of precursors serving as carbon sources by mild oxidation at atmospheric pressure. Typically, 1 g of purified sample combined with 50 mL of HCOOH and 10 mL of H2O2 in a beaker, was stirred and subjected to heating and stirring in a water bath at 80 ℃ for 4 h. After cooling, filtration of the unreacted substance was performed with a 0.45 µm membrane and a sand-core funnel, followed by dialysis for 48 h until the pH was nearly neutral. The filtrate was freeze-dried to attain brown powder. For clarity, the CDs derived from coal-based humic acid, lignite, and bituminous coal were signified as HA-CDs, L-CDs, and B-CDs, respectively. The synthesis of coal-based CDs is shown in Fig. 1 .

The microcrystalline texture was evaluated by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Germany) and Raman spectroscopy (Raman, inVia Confocal). The elemental compositions and functional groups of coal-based CDs were characterized via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Tensor 27, Bruker, Germany). The microstructure of coal-based CDs was investigated with high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The particle dimension of the coal-based CDs was determined using Nano Measurer. The ultraviolet–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra were scanned with UV-3600PLUS ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry and RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometry (Shimadzu, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent stability of coal-based carbon dots under different pH conditions
ce:section.ce:para: 3 mL buffer solution with a pH range from 1 to 14 was mixed with 5 mL coal-based CDs solution (50 mg/L) in a 10 mL volumetric flask, respectively. After sonication for 30 min, the fluorescence intensity of coal-based CDs solutions under different pH conditions (F) and the fluorescence intensity of the original coal-based CDs solution (F0) were recorded. The fluorescent stability of coal-based CDs under various pH conditions was obtained by evaluating the ratio of F/F0. Each experiment was performed thrice to ensure accuracy

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of coal-based carbon dots to Cu2+
Selectivity of coal-based carbon dots to different metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: 5 mL of coal-based carbon dots solution with a concentration of 10 mg/L was combined with 3 mL of 13 different metal ions (Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Ni2+, K+, Cr6+, Ba2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, and Cr3+) with a concentration of 100 μmol/L in 10 mL volumetric flasks numbered from 0 to 13, respectively. Finally, the volumetric flasks were filled with phosphate buffer saline. After sonication for 30 min, the fluorescence intensity of coal-based CDs solutions with 13 diverse metal ions (F) and the fluorescence intensity of the original coal-based CDs solution (F0) were recorded. The selectivity of coal-based CDs to different metal ions was obtained by evaluating the ratio of F/F0. Each experiment was executed in three parallel to guarantee accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of coal-based carbon dots to Cu2+ concentration
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of Cu2+ solutions were prepared to gauge the sensitivity of the coal-based CDs to Cu2+. 3 mL of Cu2+ solution was combined with 5 mL of coal-based CDs solution into a separate 10 mL volumetric flask. The neutrality of the mixture was maintained by the phosphate buffer saline. Following 30 min of ultrasonic dispersion, the fluorescence intensity of coal-based CDs solution with a series of Cu2+ concentrations (F) and the fluorescence intensity of the original coal-based CDs solution (F0) were recorded. The sensitivity of the coal-based CDs to various concentrations of Cu2+ was obtained by evaluating the ratio of F/F0. Each experiment was accomplished thrice in parallel to assure accuracy.

Fig. 2 shows the HRTEM images of B-CDs, L-CDs, and HA-CDs. Fig. 2(a-c) illustrate that many well-distributed sphere-like nanoparticles exhibit a uniform particle size distribution of 5–12 nm with the average sizes of 7.8 nm, 8.1 nm, and 7.6 nm, respectively, which suggests that coal-based CDs are successfully prepared. As revealed in Fig. 2(d-f), most particles show sp3 amorphous carbon and sp2 microcrystalline structure with well-resolved lattice fringes at high resolution, indicating the three coal-based CDs are rich in sp2 microcrystalline carbon (sp2 C) and sp3 amorphous carbon (sp3 C). Interestingly, B-CDs exhibit more chaotic and disordered structures in Fig. 2(d), while HA-CDs exhibit more ordered sp2 microcrystalline structures, indicating that the structure of precursor is related to the compactness of the microcrystalline carbon packing structure in CDs. It is worth pointing out that these three CDs show similar sizes, but present different lattice spacings. Specifically, the crystal plane spacing is estimated to be 0.388 nm (B-CDs), 0.365 nm (L-CDs), and 0.345 nm (HA-CDs), demonstrating that as the precursor coal-rank decreases, the macromolecular structure of the precursor is more easily destroyed by the oxidant, and the coal-based CDs with smaller size and layer spacing while retaining more sp2 microcrystalline structures from the precursor.

As the XRD patterns shown in Fig. 3 (a), the as-prepared CDs have two peaks around 42.26°±0.16° and 24.46°±1.63°, corresponding to the diffraction peak of the (100) plane and (002) plane of graphite crystal [18], which suggests that CDs are dominated by sp2 microcrystalline carbon. The (002) diffraction peaks of B-CDs (22.83°) and L-CDs (24.27°) are broad and low, shifting to the left relative to HA-CDs. This phenomenon indicates that HA-CDs possess smaller layer spacing than B-CDs and L-CDs [19]. From the Bragg equation, the corresponding layer spacings of B-CDs, L-CDs, and HA-CDs are 0.389 nm, 0.366 nm, and 0.341 nm, respectively. It is further shown that the sp2 microcrystalline structures in HA-CDs gradually tend to be denser as the layer spacing decreases, which aligns with the analysis in HRTEM. To evaluate the proportion of sp2 C and sp3 C in the CDs, the (002) peaks in XRD spectra were split by Gaussian fitting. The resulting γ and 002 peaks were obtained, respectively, where the proportion of γ peaks represented the content of sp3 C, and the proportion of (002) peaks represented the content of sp2 C [21,29]. In Fig. 3(b), the content of sp2 C increased from 57.69 % in B-CDs to 61.73 % in HA-CDs. Meanwhile, the percentage of sp3 C decreased from 42.31 % to 38.27 %. The fitting results likewise indicate that HA-CDs have higher content of sp2 microcrystalline carbon, smaller layer spacing, and more compact microcrystalline structures than L-CDs and B-CDs. The smaller layer spacing can lower the energy to form hydrogen bonds at the boundaries of the microlithic structure of HA-CDs [20,21], indicating that HA-CDs contain more atoms with high electronegativity like O and N. The Raman spectra in Fig. 3(c) illustrate that coal-based CDs exhibit a D peak representing defect structures at 1343 cm−1 and a G peak representing sp2 microcrystalline structures at 1580 cm−1 [12]. From Gaussian fitting, four sub-peaks at D4 peak (1212 cm−1), D1 peak (1369 cm−1), D3 peak (1486 cm−1), and G peak (1587 cm−1) can be derived. The relative disorder of coal-based CDs is assessed on AD1/AG. According to the calculation results, the value of AD1/AG in HA-CDs is smaller than that of B-CDs and L-CDs, indicating that HA-CDs contain more sp2 microcrystalline carbon, which is in accordance with the XRD analysis.

FTIR spectra were adopted to classify the functional groups of coal-based CDs. From Fig. 3(d), a wide absorption band at 3439 cm−1 is attributed to the presence of − OH/−NH3 [22], and peaks at 2935 cm−1 and 2886 cm−1 represent the stretching vibration of CH3 and CH2, respectively. The sharp peaks at 1713 cm−1 and 1619 cm−1 are mainly relevant to polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon structures involving C=O and C=C [23]. The broader peak near 1500 cm−1 represents the binding vibrations of aliphatic − CH3 and − CH2, and a peak at 1223 cm−1 is the existence of the C−O bond. The infrared spectra indicate the CDs are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups (C=O, C−O, −OH) and nitrogen-containing functional groups, which are primarily located at the periphery of CDs due to the atomic reactivity and the effects of spatial site resistance [24]. These functional groups not only contribute to better solubility but also provide a more effective reactive site, facilitating the participation of CDs in reactions and laying the foundation for ion detection.

The XPS spectra in Fig. 4 (a) show the coal-based CDs encompass C, O, and N elements. The spectra C 1 s display sub-peaks approximately at 284.2 eV, 284.6 eV, 285.8 eV, 286.9 eV, and 288.2 eV, matching to C=C, C−C, C−O, C=O, and O−C=O, respectively [7]. Based on the fitting results, the relative area proportion of C=C and C−C is assessed on AC=C/AC−C, and in Fig. 4(b) the value of AC=C/AC−C increased from 1.43 in B-CDs to 2.35 in HA-CDs, suggesting that HA-CDs are rich in sp2 microcrystalline structures dominated by C=C. The spectra O 1 s reveal three peaks at ∼ 531.2 eV, 532.4 eV, and 533.5 eV, centered on C=O, C−OH, and C−O−C, respectively. It is worth noting that the area ratio of the subpeaks is marked in Fig. 4 and the relative contents of part functional groups are shown in Table 2 . Especially, the contents of O−C=O groups in B-CDs (1.50 %) and L-CDs (1.50 %) are comparable. However, HA-CDs contain a significantly higher content of O−C=O of 2.09 % than that of the other two, which may enhance the adsorption capacity of HA-CDs to Cu2+. This is mainly due to the existence of carboxylic groups, differed by deprotonation, will bring out additional coordination reactions that promote chelation between HA-CDs and metal ions to form a stable coordination polymer network backbone [25]. In addition, hydroxyl groups are often considered to be strong electron-donating groups, and the content of C−OH (8.93 %) in HA-CDs is higher, which can add electronegativity to HA-CDs, thus resulting in increasing its ability to adsorb positively charged metal ions. The spectra N 1 s in Fig. 4(d) are mainly represented by pyridinic nitrogen (∼398.85 eV), pyrrolic nitrogen (∼400.20 eV), and graphitic nitrogen (∼402.43 eV). HA-CDs possess a higher content of pyridinic nitrogen (1.06 %) compared with that of the B-CDs and L-CDs, which may be related to the total amount of N element contained in the three CDs. Pyridinic nitrogen refers to a N atom attached between two C atoms on the edges of graphene with a lone electron. This indicates that the pyridinic nitrogen can be easily oxidized, conducive to the bonding between HA-CDs and metal ions. Overall, the XPS results show that the CDs contain an abundance of sp2 C as well as a moderate amount of sp3 C, oxygen-containing functional groups like C−O, C=O, −OH, and O−C=O, and nitrogen-containing functional groups like pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, in good agreement with the outcomes of FTIR.

The series of microstructure analyses shows that the precursor structure is closely related to the microstructure and surface characteristics of coal-based CDs. Specifically, Humic acid is rich in carboxyl and phenolic hydroxyl groups that are prone to protonation, thus conductive to increase the surface polarity and the hydrophilicity of HA-CDs [26]. At the same time, due to the higher content of aromatic rings in humic acid, it is possible to obtain HA-CDs with more sp2 microcrystalline carbon. Lignite has poor metamorphism and mainly contains aromatic and aliphatic compounds with alkyl side chains and oxygen-containing functional groups [27]. The loose structure can be easily destroyed to form a high yield of CDs that are rich in functional groups. The molecular structure of bituminous coal is complicated to oxidize. Under the same oxidation conditions, some easily oxidized bridge bonds are broken down first, and most of the aromatic ring structures are only partially oxidized, resulting in the obtained B-CDs containing more sp3 amorphous carbon and less sp2 microcrystalline carbon [28].

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of coal-based CDs in aqueous solutions were also recorded to study the luminescence properties. In Fig. 5 (a), B-CDs have an absorption peak at 210 nm interrelated to the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 structures [29,30]. While L-CDs and HA-CDs demonstrate strong absorption in the ultraviolet reign, with a clear and vibrant peak at 260 nm and a less pronounced peak at 290 nm, attributable to the π − π* transition of the C=C (sp2) domains and the interaction between the n − π* heterocyclic conjugated electrons and surface groups like C=O/C−OH [31]. The red-shifting trend of the absorption peak provides strong evidence for J-type aggregation in HA-CDs and L-CDs [32,33]. Similar to most literature-reported CDs, an extensive absorption in the visible light region of the three types of CDs is presumably on account of the distribution traits of diverse electron transitions [34]. Fig. 5(b-d) show the emission spectra of HA-CDs, L-CDs, and B-CDs at varied excitation wavelengths, and the optimum excitation wavelength was examined at 415 nm, 395 nm, and 375 nm, respectively. Additionally, the prepared coal-based CDs exhibit a distinct excitation wavelength-dependent feature characterized by a slow rise before a progressive decline in fluorescence intensity as the excitation wavelength increased. At the same time, it was accompanied by a shift towards longer wavelengths of the emission center. The phenomenon of excitation-dependent fluorescence in accordance with the CDs in open literature [35,36], which is probably attributable to the aromatic C=C bonds and surface defects produced by the C−OH and C=O groups that influence the band gap of CDs, optical discrimination of CDs with varying sizes [37–39].

Summarizing the above analysis, coal-based CDs were successfully prepared by atmospheric pressure and mild oxidation. Compared to other preparation techniques listed in Table 3 , this approach is eco-friendly and suitable for low-cost production. The synthetic procedure of coal-based CDs circumvents concentrated acid and high temperature, as well as toxic gases and additional procedures, except for filtration and dialysis. In addition, the yield of the CDs from the precursors is high, except for B-CDs. The reasonable explanations owe to the following factors: the basic macromolecule structure of coal-based humic acid is the aromatic ring and lipid ring, which are connected with the carboxyl group, hydroxyl group, carbonyl group, quinone group, methoxy group and other functional groups in space to form a curly long-chain polymer with certain expansion degree [40], making it be easily tailored by oxidation to obtain CDs. However, due to its susceptibility to easy oxidation, some structures may be easily destroyed by excessive oxidation during the reaction process, resulting in a low yield of HA-CDs. Lignite has a low degree of metamorphism with its macromolecular structural units mainly composed of organic molecules, such as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and fatty acids, among which polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons are the main organic components. The aromatic structural unit consists of benzene and naphthalene linked by an ether bond or fat chain to form a pore structure. This loose pore structure is easily broken-down during pyrolysis into many small carbon rings of different sizes, which is also the reason for the high yield of L-CDs [41]. Among the three raw materials, bituminous coal is the coal with a higher degree of metamorphism and a relatively complex molecular structure. Under the same conditions, some side chain groups containing a small amount of sp2 microcrystalline carbon are oxidized first, and the main carbon ring structure is not easily oxidized [42]. As the increase of oxidation degree, some sp2 structures started to peel off gradually. However, at the same time, some of the generated sp2 structures were destroyed by excessive oxidation, resulting in a low yield of B-CDs.

Microscopic characterizations show that the coal-based CDs are relatively homogeneous in particle size with a higher yield and abounding in oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups. Last but not least, such CDs predominantly consist of sp2 microcrystalline carbon and sp3 amorphous carbon, and the optical qualities of CDs are primarily contingent upon the partly sp2-hybridized carbon domains [43] and differences in sp2 microcrystalline carbon content may result in CDs with different optical properties. All unique features of the prepared coal-based CDs lay the foundation of ion detection.

The impact of different pH environments on the fluorescence was explored to assess the fluorescence stability of coal-based CDs. As shown in Fig. 6 , alteration in pH has no noteworthy impact on the emission peak center of coal-based CDs, while the fluorescence intensity of the CDs exhibits a pattern of a rise followed by a decrease in response to the variations in pH, and diminishes significantly when the pH of the solution is extremely acidic or basic. It suggests that functional groups like − OH and − COOH are prone to protonation and deprotonation [49] and exhibit high sensitivity to both low and high pH conditions, resulting in reduced fluorescence intensity of the CDs. There is minimal alteration in the fluorescence intensity in a neutral solution. In Fig. 6(d), the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs and L-CDs fluctuates dramatically as pH changes, and the fluorescence intensity of HA-CDs shows negligible changes while the pH value moves from 6 to 10, indicating exceptional fluorescence stability of the HA-CDs.

The impact of varied metal ions (Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Ni2+, K+, Cr6+, Ba2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, and Cr3+) on the fluorescence of the CDs was further assessed to examine the selectivity of coal-based CDs on metal ions. From Fig. 7 , in the same concentration of 100 µmol/L, only Cu2+ can significantly reduce the fluorescence intensity of CDs. This finding shows that the occurrence of Cu2+ notably reduced the fluorescence intensity of CDs, and the impact of other metal ions on fluorescence intensity was minimal or negligible, which revealed that the coal-based CDs could be practical to monitor Cu2+. Among the three types of CDs, HA-CDs were more sensitive to Cu2+ than L-CDs and B-CDs, and their fluorescence intensity was eroded by more than 50 % in the existence of Cu2+.

Subsequently, to further examine the selectivity of synthesized CDs to metal ions, the coal-based CDs of 10 mg/L were prepared and incubated with the double-ion-mixed solution with a concentration ratio of Cu2+ to these ions of 1:5, including Ni2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Cr6+, and Cr3+ in the presence of Cu2+. As depicted in Fig. 8 , in the attendance of two ions, the fluorescence quenching degree of CDs by Cu2+ is basically unaltered, indicating that the CDs show excellent selectivity to Cu2+ independent of other ions. On equal conditions, Cu2+ reduced the fluorescence intensity of L-CDs by only 20 %, that of B-CDs by 40 % and that of HA-CDs by 60 %, suggesting that HA-CDs show a demonstrably excellent selectivity towards Cu2+. This can be explained by the fact that a high account of − COOH, −OH, and pyridinic nitrogen provides HA-CDs more electrons for binding with Cu2+ at a higher probability than L-CDs and B-CDs [50], which can be validated with FTIR and XPS analyses.

The current study selected HA-CDs as a probe to further investigate the sensitivity to detect Cu2+. As depicted in Fig. 9 (a), the fluorescence intensity of the HA-CDs is diminished progressively with increasing concentration of Cu2+. Due to the consistent pH levels of the phosphatic buffer solution, the emission peak center remains stable. As shown in Fig. 9(b), when the Cu2+ concentration is between 0–40 µmol/L, the value of (F0 − F)/F0 has a rectilinear relationship (R2 = 0.994) with Cu2+ concentration, F and F0 representing the fluorescence intensity with and without Cu2+, respectively. The detection threshold for Cu2+ was calculated as 0.014 µmol/L in accordance with the definition prescribed by the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), meeting the standards for monitoring Cu2+ concentrations in drinking water. The aforementioned findings suggest that this approach, when utilized as a fluorescent nanoprobe for the identification of Cu2+, stands out with straightforward implementation, eco-friendly characteristics, and highly sensitive and precise detection.

To investigate the practicability of HA-CDs-based probes, the fluorescence intensity of HA-CDs at different temperatures was tested. As shown in Fig. 10 , under the excitation wavelength of 415 nm, the peak of the emission spectrum at 490 nm gradually decreases as the temperature increases from 25 ℃ to 75 ℃, but the emission center remains unchanged. At low temperatures, the fluorescence intensity of HA-CDs has a little change, while the fluorescence intensity decreases obviously when the temperatures exceed 55 ℃. This phenomenon may be due to the acceleration of molecular motion at high temperatures, leading to an increased probability of collision between the molecules of HA-CDs, which increased the likelihood of non-radiative fluorescence [51,52], thus weakening the fluorescence intensity. There is a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of HA-CDs and temperatures with a regression coefficient of 0.862, as shown in Fig. 10(b).

To further investigate the potential application, drinking water and lake water were selected to explore the HA-CDs-based probes to detect Cu2+ in actual water samples. The actual water samples were filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove impurities such as suspended matter and large particulate matter. The Cu2+ solutions with a concentration of 0–100 μmol/L were prepared using drinking water and lake water, and other operations were the same as the pure water simulation experiment. Parallel experiments were executed thrice to get an average result. Fig. 11 shows the detection performance of HA-CDs for Cu2+ in drinking water and lake water, and it can be seen that there is still a certain relationship between the fluorescence intensity of HA-CDs and the concentration of Cu2+. With the increase in the concentration of Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity decreases gradually. Fig. 11(b) and (d) display a pleasant linear relationship (R2 = 0.99) between the fluorescence intensity of HA-CDs and the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 0–40 μmol/L in two actual water samples, which is basically consistent with the detection results in pure water, indicating that the HA-CDs-based fluorescent probe can be used to detect the concentration of Cu2+ in natural water environment to a certain extent.

According to reported studies, the quenching mechanisms of CDs fall into the inner filter effect (IFE), static fluorescence quenching, and dynamic fluorescence quenching [53]. IFE usually happens when the UV–vis absorption of the quencher overlaps with the excitation or emission spectrum of CDs. In static quenching, a non-fluorescent compound is created, prompting the position of the absorption peak of CDs to change without altering the lifetime of fluorescence [54]. Dynamic quenching encompasses charge transfer and energy transfer, which can alter the fluorescence lifetime [55], like photoinduced electron transfer (PET) forming cationic and anionic radicals as well as energy gap and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) when the UV–vis absorption of the quencher overlays with the emission spectrum of CDs.

To learn about the probable principle of Cu2+ detection, the UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra were performed. In Fig. 12 (a), the UV absorption of HA-CDs exhibits attenuation and a left shift change after adding Cu2+. This indicates that static quenching may occur when significant nonfluorescent ground-state coordination or coordination complexes are present between HA-CDs and Cu2+ [56]. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of HA-CDs is notably diminished when Cu2+ is added, further indicating a non-radiative complex is formed between the CDs and Cu2+. This is consistent with the phenomenon when Wang et al. [57] used organic small molecules by high-temperature cleavage to prepare fluorescent detectors for Cu2+. In Fig. 12(b), the UV–vis absorption of Cu2+ barely shows other absorption peaks after 300 nm, and the UV–vis adsorption of Cu2+ has no spectral convergence with the fluorescence spectra of HA-CDs, which suggests the fluorescence quenching caused by IFE and FRET can’t occur [58]. In Fig. 12(c), the fluorescence lifetime before and after the addition of Cu2+ was measured, and fitted using the double exponential function (t =

). As shown in Table 4 , the average fluorescence lifetime of HA-CDs changes from 3.77 ns to 3.07 ns, indicating the emitted photons of part CDs are quenched resulting in the formation of non-radiative complex while the average lifetime is nearly invariable [59,60]. To further explore the interactions between different structures in HA-CDs and Cu2+, we simulated the orbital interactions between HA-CDs and Cu2+ using density functional theory. Fig. 13 mainly shows three bonding models of Cu2+ and oxygen-containing groups, Cu2+ and aromatic structures, and Cu2+causing defects in the structures of HA-CDs. For simplicity, these three models are named HA-CDs-Cu2+@O, HA-CDs-Cu2+@A, and HA-CDs-Cu2+@D, respectively. Based on the principles of molecular orbital theory, the reactivity properties are principally governed by the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) [61]. EHOMO represents the ability of electron-donating, and the higher the value of EHOMO , the easier the molecule is to donate electrons. ELUMO reflects the ability of electron-accepting, and the larger the value of ELUMO , the easier the molecule is to accept electrons [57]. The energy gap (Eg ) represents the reaction activity, and the smaller the energy gap, the larger the effective overlap between EHOMO and ELUMO . In the three combination modes, the EHOMO values decrease in the order of HA-CDs-Cu2+@O>HA-CDs-Cu2+@A>HA-CDs-Cu2+@D, and the ELUMO values are in the order of HA-CDs-Cu2+@A>HA-CDs-Cu2+@O>HA-CDs-Cu2+@D. According to Table 5 , the energy gap (Eg ) shows a decreasing trend, indicating the formation of non-fluorescent complexes. The binding modes HA-CDs-Cu2+@O led to the smallest Eg , which indicates the highest reaction activity between oxygen-containing functional groups in HA-CDs and Cu2+, and the strongest bond formed. This suggests that the electrons in HA-CDs more easily undergo excitation leaps after the addition of Cu2+, and it is speculated that the fluorescence quenching may be caused by the PET between oxygen-containing groups on the surface of HA-CDs and Cu2+, as shown in Fig. 14 .

Based on the above microscopic characterizations and analysis, the possible causes of fluorescence quenching may be listed below: (1) Cu2+ shows higher absorption affinity for carbon nanostructures than other metal ions [62]. This is the precondition for fluorescence quenching. (2) A fluorescence quenching process caused by PET may occur. HA-CDs contain a high number of oxygen-containing groups (−COOH, −OH), and there is an energy gap between HA-CDs and Cu2+ which led to the energy level splitting of the d-orbital of Cu2+, thus resulting in a new orbital available for excitation electrons of CDs to return to the ground state [53]. This process significantly inhibits the radiative form of the electron-leap process, which ultimately leads to the formation of non-radiative complexes and fluorescence quenching [63], cohering with the description provided in the research of Zhang et al. [64]. (3) The HA-CDs possess plenty of pyridinic nitrogen, which has lone electrons and high activity, resulting in the aggregation of HA-CDs around Cu2+ forming the copper-ammonia complexes via electron transfer, which correlates with the quenching in fluorescence.

Herein, a series of fluorescent coal-based CDs were successfully synthesized via mild oxidation at atmospheric pressure by employing different carbon sources, including coal-based humic acid, lignite, and bituminous coal. Coal-based CDs show uniform particle sizes with an average diameter of 7.6–8.1 nm, and contain sp2 microcrystalline structures and sp3 amorphous carbon, as well as possess abundant oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups. Optical assessments demonstrate that the fluorescence of coal-based CDs exhibit bright fluorescence along with typical excitation wavelength-dependent characteristics. HA-CDs exhibit superior fluorescence stability under different pH environments and remain excellent selectivity to Cu2+ when other ions are present because of the appropriate proportion of sp2 C, a higher content of − COOH, −OH, and pyridinic nitrogen. At the same concentration of Cu2+ of 100 μmol/L, the fluorescence intensity of HA-CDs, L-CDs, and B-CDs can be diminished by 60 %, 20 %, and 40 %, respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of HA-CDs shows brilliant sensitivity to Cu2+, displaying a closely and outstanding lined association with concentration of Cu2+ in the scope of 0–40 μmol/L and the detection limit of sensitivity of 0.014 μmol/L, which could meet the standard for monitoring Cu2+ in potable water. The possible quenching of fluorescence is due to electron transfer between − COOH, −OH, and nitrogen-containing groups on the outward of HA-CDs and Cu2+. The current study not only contributes to the low-cost production of CDs, but also affords a valuable conception for design of Cu2+ probe.
